hero.tagline: Utah's small claims courts offer a streamlined process for resolving monetary disputes up to $20,000, with simplified procedures and no requirement for legal representation.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in district court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: As of January 1, 2025, the maximum claim amount increased to $20,000

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $20,000, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the small claims limit. You must either reduce your claim to $20,000 or file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Utah allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed, with a statute of limitations of 6 years for written contracts and 4 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $15,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never completed the work.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $5,000, which they promised to repay in a year, but they failed to do so.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness testimony.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent $10,000 to a colleague with the understanding it would be repaid in 6 months, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring any written agreement or proof of the loan and attempts to collect.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $8,000 over 12 months but stopped making payments after 3 months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided consulting services worth $7,500 to a client who has not paid the invoice issued 3 months ago.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $3,000 on their account with your business and has ignored payment requests.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring account statements, terms of agreement, and records of payment attempts.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent over the past 3 months and has vacated the property without notice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any communication with the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $2,000 with a check that was returned for insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Demand letter required. 30-day notice to the issuer before filing. Bring the bounced check and notice of dishonor.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $12,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now you're seeking repayment from them.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the loan agreement and proof of payments you've made on their behalf.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Utah's small claims courts handle various consumer disputes, with specific statutes providing remedies and protections.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,500 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $500 but failed to perform the agreed-upon tasks satisfactorily.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement, payment proof, and evidence of poor service.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $3,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and they refuse to refund or redo the work.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Bring the repair invoice, payment proof, and any communication with the mechanic.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $10,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway through.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and evidence of incomplete work.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: You paid $2,500 for catering services for an event, but the vendor never showed up and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from service date. Bring the service agreement, payment proof, and any communication with the vendor.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continues to charge your credit card $50 monthly despite multiple cancellation requests.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from first unauthorized charge. Provide bank statements, cancellation requests, and company responses.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $8,000 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the sales contract, payment proof, and any communication with the dealer.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to loss
claims.example: A retailer advertised a product for $500, but charged you $700 at checkout without honoring the advertised price.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement, receipt, and any communication with the retailer.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Utah's small claims courts address various property damage claims, with specific statutes outlining the procedures and limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver ran a red light and caused $6,000 in damage to your car.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report, repair estimates, and any witness statements.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $2,500 in damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $4,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement, move-in/move-out inspection reports, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,500 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your laptop with a repair shop, and they returned it with a cracked screen, causing $800 in damage.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from damage date. Bring the service agreement, photos of the damage, and any communication with the shop.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Utah's small claims courts handle monetary disputes between landlords and tenants, excluding eviction proceedings.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,200 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: No itemized list for deposit deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit but didn't provide an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to maintain habitable premises
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it, forcing you to spend $2,000 on alternative accommodations.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from issue occurrence. Provide the lease agreement, photos of the issue, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's personal property
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord kept your furniture worth $3,000, claiming it was abandoned.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement, inventory of items, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your belongings.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from eviction date. Provide the lease agreement, photos of the changed locks, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord failed to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid the last month's rent in advance, but after moving out early, the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months of rent totaling $3,000 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement, rent ledger, and any communication with the tenant.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater costing $600 and deducted it from your rent, but the landlord is now demanding full payment.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Provide the lease agreement, repair receipts, and any communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Utah's small claims courts handle various employment-related disputes, with specific statutes outlining the procedures and limitations.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but has refused to pay.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from wage due date. Provide pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer failed to provide your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from wage due date. Bring resignation letter, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from commission due date. Provide sales records, commission agreements, and any communication with the employer.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime last month but were only paid your regular hourly rate.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from wage due date. Bring timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication with the employer.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Utah's small claims courts address various personal claims, with specific statutes outlining the procedures and limitations.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on an unmarked wet floor in a store, resulting in $5,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from injury date. Provide medical records, bills, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,200 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from demand for return. Provide proof of ownership, loan agreement, and any communication with the borrower.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $800 bicycle without permission and refuses to return it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from theft date. Provide proof of ownership, police report, and any communication with the neighbor.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Utah's small claims courts handle various vehicle-related disputes, with specific statutes outlining the procedures and limitations.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs you didn't authorize.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from repair date. Provide the repair invoice, payment proof, and any communication with the mechanic.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company charged you $300 for its return.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from tow date. Provide photos of the parking spot, tow notice, and any communication with the towing company.
claims.damage_boost: Possible recovery of attorney fees and up to 3x actual damages under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims courts in Utah do not have jurisdiction over eviction proceedings.
Disputes involving the title to real property are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims courts.
Claims involving defamation, such as libel or slander, are not handled in small claims courts.
Professional malpractice claims require more complex proceedings than small claims courts can provide.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are outside the jurisdiction of small claims courts.
Disputes involving wills, estates, and trusts are not handled in small claims courts.
Small claims courts do not have jurisdiction over claims against federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are beyond the scope of small claims courts.
Small claims courts can only award monetary damages, not equitable relief.
These claims are handled by the Utah Labor Commission, not small claims courts.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in Utah's small claims courts; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases, such as bad check claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases involving violations of the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act or bad check claims.

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing depends on the specific Justice Court; check with the court where you plan to file.

service_fees.notes: Fees vary by county; contact the local sheriff's office for exact amounts.
Fees depend on current postal rates.
Fees vary by provider; contact local process servers for quotes.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees; service fees may still apply.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fee is added to the judgment against the loser.

other_fees.name: Appeal filing
other_fees.notes: An additional $10 fee is payable to the justice court at the time the small claims notice of appeal is filed.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention. For bad check claims, a 30-day notice is required before filing.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice must be sent to the issuer of a bad check before filing a claim, demanding payment of the check amount plus applicable fees.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific demand requirement for wage claims, but it's advisable to request payment in writing before filing.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination when withholding any portion of a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Under the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act, consumers may recover attorney fees and up to three times actual damages for deceptive practices.

forms.name: Affidavit and Summons
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by stating the claim and summoning the defendant to court.

forms.name: Counter Affidavit
forms.description: Allows the defendant to file a counterclaim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to appeal a small claims judgment to the district court.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed by the plaintiff to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Motion to Set Aside Judgment
forms.description: Filed to request the court to vacate or modify a judgment.

forms.name: Writ of Garnishment
forms.description: Filed to initiate garnishment of the defendant's wages or bank accounts to satisfy a judgment.

forms.name: Writ of Execution
forms.description: Filed to authorize the seizure of the defendant's property to satisfy a judgment.

forms.name: Affidavit of Impecuniosity
forms.description: Filed to request a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Court clerk sends the papers via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual serves the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Utah's long-arm statute and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, the court may allow service by publication as a last resort.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document all attempts and consider alternative methods like service by publication.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must present evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the decision within 10 days.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys, but it is not required.
hearing.format: Bench trial with each side presenting their case; typically lasts 30-60 minutes.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk as soon as possible before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on the day of the hearing; check with the local court.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the district court, as if the original trial had not occurred.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within the same 10-day window.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds and remits a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment targeting the debtor's bank account, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and remits funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains sufficient funds in bank accounts.
exemptions: Certain amounts may be exempt under state law

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File an abstract of judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's real property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property and intends to sell or refinance.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: File a writ of execution with the court, serve the debtor, and law enforcement seizes and sells non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.
exemptions: Certain personal property may be exempt under state law

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; renewal extends the judgment for another 8 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a statement with the state.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors can send a designated employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face limitations in enforcing contracts in court.

pitfalls.title: Filing in the wrong venue
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing in the incorrect court can lead to dismissal or transfer, causing delays and additional costs.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure you file in the county where the defendant resides or where the claim arose.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Claims exceeding $20,000 are not eligible for small claims court and must be filed in a higher court.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify that your claim amount, excluding interest and court costs, does not exceed $20,000.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be aware of the specific time limits for your type of claim and file accordingly.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can delay proceedings or result in dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process carefully, using approved methods.

pitfalls.title: Not bringing necessary evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can weaken your case and lead to an unfavorable outcome.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather and bring all relevant documents, photos, and witness information to court.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect on a judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with post-judgment collection methods and be prepared to use them.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims monetary limit
recent_changes.description: The maximum amount for small claims cases increased from $15,000 to $20,000.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: It typically takes at least 45 days to get a judgment in small claims court, depending on the court's schedule and the specifics of your case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: As of January 1, 2025, the maximum amount you can sue for in Utah's small claims court is $20,000, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear at the scheduled hearing, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment by filing a notice of appeal within 10 days of the judgment.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A small claims judgment in Utah is valid for 8 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: Utah's small claims courts have jurisdiction over monetary disputes up to $20,000.
Eviction proceedings are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in district court.
The statute of limitations for written contracts is 6 years, while for oral contracts it is 4 years.
Attorney fees may be recoverable in cases involving violations of the Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act.
A 30-day written notice is required before filing a claim for a bad check.

interesting_findings: Utah's small claims courts offer a streamlined process for resolving monetary disputes up to $20,000, with simplified procedures and no requirement for legal representation.

sources.label: Utah Rules of Small Claims Procedure
Service of Process — Utah Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4
Appeals from Small Claims — Utah Rules of Small Claims Procedure Rule 12
Utah Consumer Sales Practices Act, Utah Code § 13-11-1 et seq.
Civil Liability for Dishonored Checks — Utah Code § 7-15-1 et seq.
Utah Fit Premises Act (Security Deposit, Repairs, Lockout) — Utah Code § 57-22-1 et seq.
Utah Payment of Wages Act — Utah Code § 34-28-1 et seq.
